GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: DISTRIBUTION TO A DONOR ADVISED FUND
GeneralExplanationGrp.FormAndLineReferenceDesc: Form 990-PF, Part VII, Section A Line12
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: THE W.K. KELLOGG FOUNDATION MADE GRANTS DURING THE TAX YEAR TO VARIOUS DONOR ADVISED FUNDS (DAFS) AT COMMUNITY FOUNDATIONS, COLLECTIVELY AMOUNTING TO LESS THAN 1% OF THE FOUNDATION'S TOTAL GRANTS PAID DURING THE YEAR. THESE GRANTS ALLOWED FOUNDATION TRUSTEES TO GAIN ADDITIONAL EXPERIENCE IN GRANT-MAKING, AS WELL AS LENDING THEIR EXPERTISE TO COMMUNITY FOUNDATIONS, BY RECOMMENDING PUBLIC CHARITIES TO RECEIVE THE DAFS DISTRIBUTIONS. ALL OF THE DAFS ARE COMMITTED FOR PURPOSES DESCRIBED IN SECTION 170(C)(2)(B) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE AND ARE CONTROLLED BY THE COMMUNITY FOUNDATIONS THAT MAINTAIN THE DAFS. AS SUCH, THESE GRANTS TO THE DAFS ARE TREATED AS QUALIFYING DISTRIBUTIONS BY THE FOUNDATION.

GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: States to which the foundation reports or with which it is registered
GeneralExplanationGrp.FormAndLineReferenceDesc: Form 990-PF, Part VII, Section A Line8b
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: The state of Louisiana does not require that a copy of the Form 990-PF be filed with the state Attorney General. The W.K. Kellogg Foundation ("Foundation") reports income and deductions from partnership investments as Unrelated Business taxable Income ("UBTI") on Form 990-T. These partnerships have communicated the amount of state UBTI via Schedule K-1 footnotes or other detailed schedules provided, and therefore the Foundation files a state tax return and copy of the Form 990-T in Louisiana.

THE W.K. KELLOGG FOUNDATION ("FOUNDATION") IS THE SOLE BENEFICIARY OF THE W.K. KELLOGG FOUNDATION TRUST ("TRUST"). AS A RESULT, DISBURSEMENTS ON THIS FORM 990-PF REFLECT DISTRIBUTIONS MADE FROM THE FOUNDATION'S OWN ASSETS AS WELL AS THE REDISTRIBUTION OF FUNDS RECEIVED FROM THE TRUST. THE NET ASSETS REFLECTED ON THE BALANCE SHEET IN PART II OF THE FORM 990-PF ARE THOSE OF THE FOUNDATION ONLY.

GeneralExplanationGrp.IdentifierTxt: QUALIFICATION UNDER SEC. 4940(E) FOR REDUCED TAX ON NET INVESTMENT INCOME
GeneralExplanationGrp.MediumExplanationTxt: TAX YEAR 2016: $432,567,167 PART XII, LINE 6 (411,465,510) PART XIII LINE 4C $21,101,657 SEE NOTE* TAX YEAR 2015: $397,982,572 PART XII, LINE 6 (388,479,439) PART XIII LINE 4C $9,503,133 SEE NOTE* TAX YEAR 2014: $379,680,402 PART XII, LINE 6 (355,934,030) PART XIII LINE 4C $23,746,372 SEE NOTE* TAX YEAR 2013: $376,500,492 PART XII, LINE 6 (353,436,899) PART XIII LINE 4C $23,063,593 SEE NOTE* TAX YEAR 2012: $342,508,532 PART XII, LINE 6 (314,519,468) PART XIII LINE 4C $27,989,064 SEE NOTE* *NOTE: AMOUNTS RECEIVED FROM W.K. KELLOGG FOUNDATION TRUST, TREATED AS DISTRIBUTIONS OUT OF CORPUS TO SATISFY REQUIREMENTS IMPOSED BY TREASURY REGULATION SECTION 53.4942(A)-3(C).

LINE 1: Truth, Racial Healing & Transformation (TRHT) / National Day of Racial Healing (NDORH) - The WKKF-led TRHT enterprise is a comprehensive, national and community-based process to plan for and bring about transformational and sustainable change, and to address the historic and contemporary effects of racism. TRHT seeks to unearth and jettison the deeply held, and often unconscious, beliefs created by racism - the main one being the belief in a "hierarchy of human value." The National Day of Racial Healing (NDORH) is a part of WKKF's TRHT effort. The day was established in 2017 by more than 550 US leaders who wanted to set aside a day to take action together and: 1) Find ways to reinforce and honor our common humanity and create space to celebrate the distinct differences that make our communities vibrant; 2) Acknowledge that there are still deep racial divisions in America that must be overcome and healed; and 3) Commit to engaging people from all racial, ethnic, religious and identity groups in genuine efforts to increase understanding, communication, caring and respect for one another. LINE 2: BATTLE CREEK PUBLIC SCHOOLS INITIATIVE - This initiative will span five years and attempts to improve student achievement. As a first chapter in this five-year endeavor, will improve the district and community. This will increase collaboration with the district and community leaders as well as students and parents. LINE 3: ALIGNED ACTION - Building out a framework for collaboration around knowledge, data, and learning is complex and may best be done in collaboration with select other funders and technology companies. We are in the early days of coalition building around a possible collaborative effort. We are exploring what might be possible if a group of funders and technology companies came together on behalf of changemakers, and worked to encourage the creation of a more-connected digital ecosystem to support their work. Representatives from our exploration partners form what we refer to as our Aligned Action Core Team. Thus far, the primary focus of the Core Team has been on Information, Systems and Technology leaders from peer foundations. As IST leaders acknowledge that a collaborative approach is feasible and has distinct benefits, they are encouraged to engage Programmatic colleagues and bring them into the discussion.

LINE 1: INNER CITY CHRISTIAN FEDERATION - SINGLE FAMILY HOMES PORTFOLIO INITIATIVE: SUPPORT ASSET BUILDING AND FAMILY ECONOMIC SECURITY THROUGH A LOAN TO SECURE SINGLE-FAMILY PROPERTIES ENSURING THE PRESERVATION OF AFFORDABLE HOUSING LINE 2: CITY OF BATTLE CREEK - HERITAGE TOWER REDEVELOPMENT PROJECT: SUPPORT COLLABORATIVE REVITALIZATION EFFORTS TO CREATE QUALITY EMPLOYMENT AND ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT OPPORTUNITIES BY REDEVELOPING DOWNTOWN BATTLE CREEK'S HISTORIC HERITAGE TOWER LINE 3: NORTHERN GREAT LAKES INITIATIVE - BATTLE CREEK SMALL BUSINESS LOAN FUND ($1,000,000): IMPROVE ECONOMIC SECURITY AND DRIVE JOB CREATION IN THE CITY OF BATTLE CREEK BY INCREASING ACCESS TO CAPITAL FOR SMALL BUSINESSES AND ENTREPRENEURS OF COLOR COMMUNITY SERVICES UNLIMITED - SUPPORT HEALTHY FOOD ACCESS AND A COMMUNITY ORGANIC MARKET ($1,000,000): EXPAND HEALTHY FOOD ACCESS AND A COMMUNITY ORGANIC MARKET BY SUPPORTING THE CONSTRUCTION AND OPERATIONS OF THE PAUL ROBESEN COMMUNITY WELLNESS CENTER RIO GRANDE VALLEY MULTIBANK CORPORATION - COMMUNITY LOAN CENTER SUPPORT ($500,000): PROVIDE ALTERNATIVES TO PREDATORY LENDING BY INVESTING LOAN CAPITAL TO ENABLE NEW COMMUNITY LOAN CENTERS TO EXPAND TO NEW MARKETS DETROIT DEVELOPMENT FUND - ENTREPRENEURS OF COLOR ($500,000): IMPROVE ACCESS TO CAPITAL FOR SMALL, MINORITY-OWNED CONTRACTORS AND NEIGHBORHOOD BUSINESSES WITH DEMONSTRATED GROWTH POTENTIAL BY PROVIDING PROGRAM-RELATED INVESTMENT LOAN CAPITAL MACRO CONTENT FUND I LLC - NARRATIVE CHANGE FOR AFRICAN AMERICAN, LATINO AND MULTICULTURAL CONSUMERS ($75,014): SUPPORT EFFORTS TO ADVANCE TRUTH, RACIAL HEALING AND TRANSFORMATION BY INCREASING OPPORTUNITIES FOR NARRATIVE CHANGE AND SHIFTING THE BALANCE OF RACIAL AND GENDER EQUITY AND DIVERSITY IN TRADITIONAL MEDIA
